title: NOC 51100 – Librarians | GTR Immigration
description: Librarians under NOC 51100 are professional information specialists responsible for managing library collections, providing research support, and developing access systems for knowledge resources across Canada. Classified under TEER 1, this occupation usually requires a university degree at the master’s level. Librarians are commonly employed by:

classification.teer: Classification LevelDetailsBroad Occupation Category5 – Occupations in art, culture, recreation and sportTEER1 – Occupations usually require a university degreeMajor Group51 – Professional occupations in art and cultureSub-Major Group511 – Professional occupations in art and cultureMinor Group5110 – Librarians, archivists, conservators and curatorsNOC Code51100

duties: Librarians perform some or all of the following duties:
Recommend acquisition of books, periodicals, audio-visual, and digital materials
Select, classify, catalogue, and manage library collections
Prepare bibliographies, indexes, and research guides
Develop systems for accessing library collections
Conduct manual and online reference searches
Develop taxonomies and information classification systems
Provide specialized programs for children, seniors, and other groups
Conduct orientation sessions and training programs
Supervise library technicians, assistants, and clerical staff
employment_requirements: A master’s degree in library science, library and information studies, or a program accredited by the American Library Association is required
Professional certification may be required in certain provinces
Experience in research or information services may be preferred
salary_prospects: $48,100 – $103,600 per year,depending on province, employer type, and specialization.
With 5,000 projected job openings and slightly fewer job seekers (4,800), NOC 51100 reflects stable and balanced employment prospects.
Increased reliance on digital research systems
Opportunities are particularly strong in provinces such as British Columbia, Alberta, Ontario, and Quebec.
